coredata.dc:title: Solid-phase synthesized iron-mediated carbon dots nanozymes with fluorescence and peroxidase-like activity for multi-mode analysis of dopamine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Hua
Li, Hongli
Zhang, Yutong
Wang, Xin
Li, Zihan
Zhou, Mingyang
Lei, Yongqian
Guo, Pengran
Liu, Jian
Yang, Shenghong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes open a new horizon for fabricating multi-mode analysis systems, but their practical applications are still limited by complicated synthesis strategy and single-function nature. Herein, a new type of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots nanozyme was developed through simple pyrolyzing the solid mixture of ferrous bis-glycinate and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride. The Fe,N-CDs exhibit good optical properties that can emit obvious blue fluorescence and possess excellent peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species. Based on the fluorescence and peroxidase-like activity of the Fe,N-CDs, a multi-mode detection platform for DA was constructed effectively, including naked eye colorimetric, UV–Vis ratiometric absorption, fluorescence quenching, and scanometric. The limit of detection can reach 10 nM, and the calibration curve was linear up to 30 μM. The multi-mode sensing system was successfully applied for the accurate determination of DA in various actual samples with a satisfactory recovery of 89.0–118.0 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots-based nanozyme
Peroxidase-like activity
Multi-mode platform
Dopamine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Activity and kinetics study of the peroxidase-like of Fe,N-CDs
Peroxidase-like activity and enzyme kinetics of Fe,N-CDs

Research Fund Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Measurement and Emergency Test Technology (No.KF2024004), the GDAS\u2019 Project of Science and Technology Development (No.2023GDASZH-2023010103).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), particle size distribution (B), XRD pattern (C), and FTIR spectrum of Fe,N-CDs (D). The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (E), N 1 s (F) and O 1 s (G) and Fe 2p (H).

UV–Vis absorption spectra and visual color changes of the catalyzed oxidation of TMB (A). Electron spin-resonance spectra of TEMP-1 O2 , DMPO-·OH and DMPO-O2 ·- for Fe,N-CDs (B). The steady-state kinetic assay of Fe,N-CDs-catalyzed oxidation of TMB by H2 O2 (C,D). The steady-state kinetic assay of Fe,N-CDs-catalyzed decomposition of H2 O2 (E,F).

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of Fe,N-CDs (A). The emission spectra of Fe,N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B). The UV–Vis spectra of Fe,N-CDs-DA/TMB, H2 O2 Fe,N-CDs-TMB-H2 O2 and Fe,N-CDs-TMB-DA-H2 O2 (C). The fluorescence spectra of Fe,N-CDs, Fe,N-CDs-DA, Fe,N-CDs- H2 O2 and Fe,N-CDs-DA-H2 O2 (D).

UV–Vis absorbance spectra of Fe,N-CDs-TMB-H2 O2 system after adding different concentrations DA (A), the linear relation between A(450nm) /A(652nm) ratio values and DA concentrations (B). The fluorescence spectra of Fe,N-CDs-DA-H2 O2 with various concentrations of DA presence (D), the linear relation between the fluorescence intensities and DA concentrations (E). A diagram of a smartphone-based DA detection platform (G), and a linear calibration diagram of DA concentrations and G/B values in scanometric analysis mode (H). The effects of potentially interfering organic compounds (30 μM) and metal ions (100 μM) on the UV–Vis ratiometric absorption detection (C), fluorescent quenching detection (F), and scanometric analysis of DA (I). The selectivity experiment concentration of DA for UV–Vis ratiometric absorption/fluorescent quenching detection and scanometric analysis is 30 and 20 μM, respectively.

Fluorescence microscopy images (A1-C1) and their corresponding bright-field transmission images (A2-C2) and merged images (A3-C3) of A549 cells. Stained with Fe,N-CDs (A1-A3), pretreated with 20 μM (B1-B3) and 50 μM (C1-C3) DA and then stained with Fe,N-CDs-H2 O2 .
Schematic diagram of the solid phase synthesis of Fe,N-CDs and its application in multi-mode DA detection.
The detection performance of scanometric method of the proposed sensing platform for multiple actual samples spiked with different amounts of DA.

head.ce:title: Solid-phase synthesized iron-mediated carbon dots nanozymes with fluorescence and peroxidase-like activity for multi-mode analysis of dopamine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors, Hua Wei and Hongli Li contributed equally to this manuscript and should be considered as co-first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) nanozymes open a new horizon for fabricating multi-mode analysis systems, but their practical applications are still limited by complicated synthesis strategy and single-function nature. Herein, a new type of iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots nanozyme was developed through simple pyrolyzing the solid mixture of ferrous bis-glycinate and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride. The Fe,N-CDs exhibit good optical properties that can emit obvious blue fluorescence and possess excellent peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species. Based on the fluorescence and peroxidase-like activity of the Fe,N-CDs, a multi-mode detection platform for DA was constructed effectively, including naked eye colorimetric, UV–Vis ratiometric absorption, fluorescence quenching, and scanometric. The limit of detection can reach 10 nM, and the calibration curve was linear up to 30 μM. The multi-mode sensing system was successfully applied for the accurate determination of DA in various actual samples with a satisfactory recovery of 89.0–118.0 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is an irreplaceable neurotransmitter and hormone in the human body, playing a very important role in regulating working memory and motivational behavior [1,2]. The DA level in vivo is associated with the emotions of people, which have been used in clinical practice for the treatment of depression. Abnormalities in DA levels can cause a series of neuropathic diseases, including schizophrenia, senile dementia, hyperactivity disorder, parkinsonism, premature death, etc [3,4]. In particular, DA also aroused serious attention in the food safety field due to its unique decomposition effect on protein deposition and fat, and the immoderate intake of DA from food is intended to cause a series of adverse reactions associated with neuroexcitability [5]. In this context, the accurate determination of DA level can provide an important basis for food-borne disease prevention, diagnosis, and treatment [6].

Currently, several effective analytical technologies have been developed for detecting DA, including electrochemistry [7], chemiluminescence [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], and high-performance liquid chromatography [10]. Recently, colorimetric and fluorescent methods have been highly advocated as efficient alternatives due to their distinct color change, shorter time consumption, low cost, convenient operation, and higher sensitivity [11]. The multi-mode analysis system with internal cross-validation advantage based on optical signal change is much more suitable for the fast and reliable detection of DA [12]. However, dual-functional sensing substrates that can be applied for constructing multi-mode detection systems directly are still rare up to now. Thus, the development of versatile sensing materials for constructing a multi-mode analysis platform of DA is still urgent to satisfy the demand of food safety and disease diagnosis.

The emergence of nanozymes has provided a new horizon for colorimetric detection based on catalytic chromogenic strategy due to their strong tolerance, good stability, and adjustable activity compared with natural enzymes [13]. Aiming to various analysis demands, diverse types of nanozymes have been designed, such as metal–organic frameworks [14], metal nanoparticles [15], metallic oxide nanoparticles [16], and covalent organic frameworks [17]. These nanozymes are all useful for colorimetric analysis but compromise fluorescence detection because of inferior photoluminescence performance, which commonly needs the assistance of extra dyes [18]. Besides, the practical applications of conventional nanozymes still suffer from high biotoxicity and low environmental friendliness. Hence, the development of biocompatibility new nanozymes is of significance.

As the most diverse category of emerging nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are widely known for their exceptional biocompatibility, photoluminescence properties, low cytotoxicity, cost-effective precursors, stability, and economical preparation [19,20]. Numerous CDs-based fluorescent analysis systems also have been developed for the detection of metal ions [21], bioactive molecules [22], organic pollution [23], etc. Unfortunately, the construction of multi-mode sensing systems using CDs as substrates is still limited by their single fluorescence characteristics. The in-depth investigation of nanozymes and the effectiveness of the heteroatom doping strategy provides an unprecedented opportunity for designing versatile CDs [24]. Rational doping of metal atoms, such as Cu, Co, Fe, can endow CDs with certain catalytic activity, thereby further promoting the advance of multi-mode analysis [25]. However, most CDs synthesis strategies currently relying on solvothermal reactions are not conducive to large-scale applications due to their disadvantages of prolonged reaction time and excessive solvent consumption [26]. Therefore, obtaining multifunction CDs and achieving dual-mode analysis through optimizing the synthesis route holds significant implications.

The potential advantages of nanozymes and CDs sparked us to develop a facile synthesis method for preparing bifunctional CDs nanozymes with both fluorescence and catalytic performance. In this study, we proposed an effective solid-phase synthesis strategy for constructing iron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Fe,N-CDs) nanozyme with both peroxidase-like properties and fluorescence properties through simple pyrolyzing the solid mixture of ferrous glycine and ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (Scheme 1 ). The designed Fe,N-CDs show bright blue fluorescence with an emission center at 456 nm as well as obvious peroxidase-like activity that can catalytic H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (1O2, ⋅OH and O2⋅-). Based on the versatile properties of Fe,N-CDs, a multi-mode analysis platform of DA was constructed successfully, including naked eye colorimetric, UV–Vis ratiometric-absorption, fluorescence quenching, and scanometric method. The proposed sensing system exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity in detecting DA, enabling the successful determination of DA in real samples. This study not only establishes a unique multi-mode analysis platform for DA detection but also provides insights into the design and manufacture of multi-mode optical and portable sensors through CDs-based nanozymes.

Ferrous bisglycinate (Fe-Gly), ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (EDA·2HCl), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), salicylic acid (SA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), acetic acid (HAc), and sodium acetate (NaAc) were purchased from Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) was acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ·cm was used in the whole experiment.

The morphology and particle size of Fe,N-CDs were characterized by a Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Netherlands). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) was used to characterize the crystal structure of Fe,N-CDs. The functional groups sand element compositions of Fe,N-CDs were represented by NEXUS 670 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet, USA) and EscaLab Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The UV-2800S UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shanghai Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and F97Pro fluorescent spectrophotometer (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) were used for recording UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, respectively. Electron paramagnet resonance (EPR, EMXplus-6/1, Bruker, Germany) spectrum was used to characterize the reactive oxygen species in the system.

For the synthesis of Fe,N-CDs, 0.1 g Fe-Gly and 0.1 g EDA·2HCl were ground and mixed evenly in an agate mortar, then the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined autoclave and directly solid-phase pyrolyzed for 2 h at 180 °C. The obtained solid products were collected and re-dispersed after the reaction finished. After dialyzing for 12 h (MWCO = 500), the Fe,N-CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying. As control experiments, the synthesis of Fe-CDs and N-CDs was carried out through the same procedure as the above-mentioned by using Fe-Gly and EDA·2HCl as raw materials, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity and kinetics study of the peroxidase-like of Fe,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Fe,N-CDs, Fe-CDs, and N-CDs was evaluated by catalyzing H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and oxidizing the TMB to oxTMB. The typical absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm was measured after adding TMB (10 mM, 30 μL), H2O2 (1 M, 15 μL) and Fe,N-CDs/Fe-CDs/N-CDs (0.01 g·mL−1, 6 μL) to 2.9 mL HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 3.8) for reacting 5 min.

To evaluate the kinetics of Fe,N-CDs, the effect of the concentrations of TMB, H2O2 and Fe,N-CDs on the catalysis system was optimized first. The Beer-Lambert Law (Eq. (1)) was used to calculate the initial reaction rates (ν0) (with an ε = 39000 M−1·cm−1) and the Michaelis-Menten equation was fitted (Eq. (2)). Moreover, the maximum reaction velocity (V max) and Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) obtained from Eq. (2) were further verified using the linear double-reciprocal graph (Lineweaver-Burk plot, Eq. (3)).
(1)
ν 0
=
Δ A
ε b
Δ t
(2)

1

V
max

Three analysis modes including the UV–Vis ratiometric absorption, fluorescence quenching, and scanometric were fabricated for the detection of DA. For ratiometric-absorption mode, different concentrations DA were added to the HAc-NaAc buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 3.8) that contains 20 μg·mL−1 Fe,N-CDs, 0.05 mM TMB and 10 mM H2O2. The absorbances at 450 nm and 652 nm of the system were measured after incubation for 13 min at room temperature to further calculate the A(450nm)/A(652nm) values. The scanometric mode was performed by reading the RGB values of solution images in a 96-well plate, and the G/B values under different concentrations of DA presence were calculated to establish a standard curve. For fluorescence quenching detection, the mixed HAc-NaAc buffer solutions (0.1 M, pH = 3.8) with 20 μg·mL−1 Fe,N-CDs and 10 mM H2O2 were prepared firstly, then different concentrations of DA were added to the mixtures in turn and incubation for 10 min. The fluorescent intensities at 456 nm under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm were recorded.

To investigate the selectivity of the analysis system, the experiment results were collected in the presence of potential interfering substances, including common reducing substances such as cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), multi-amino acids: lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), arginine (Arg), glutamic acid (Glu), serine (Ser), citric acid (CA), tartaric acid (TA), urea, lactose (Lac), glucose (Gluc), and a series of common metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+ and Al3+.

Medical dopamine hydrochloride injection (10 mg·mL−1) were selected as non-spiked standard samples to verify the accuracy of the developed method, and no extra pretreatment was performed except a dilution using HAc-NaAc buffer before analysis. Several real food samples, including fetal bovine serum, calf serum, eggs and quail eggs were used to check the practicability of established analysis methods. The fetal bovine serum and calf serum samples were obtained from Biological Industries Israel Belt Haemek Ltd. Eggs and quail eggs were purchased from the local Yinzuo Supermarket in Changqing Guyun Lake Street (Jinan, China). The recovery experiments were carried out to check the reliability of the proposed methods by adding different concentrations of DA to the pre-treated samples.

The Fe,N-CDs with both photoluminescence and peroxidase-like activity were synthesized through simple pyrolyzing the solid mixture of Fe-Gly and EDA·2HCl. As shown in Fig. S1, the Fe,N-CDs not only can emit blue fluorescence but also exhibit obvious peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze the oxidation reaction of TMB to oxTMB by H2O2. The Fe-CDs, synthesized by single Fe-Gly, just possess enzyme-like activity, while the N-CDs obtained from EDA·2HCl cannot show significant fluorescence and catalysis properties. Thus, the Fe-doping can endow the Fe,N-CDs with good peroxidase-like activity and the N-doping extra provided by EDA·2HCl can effectively improve the fluorescence performance of products. The excellent optical property and peroxidase-like activity of Fe,N-CDs should be attributed to the synergistic reaction effect of Fe-Gly and EDA·2HCl. The reaction conditions including reactants mass ratio, reaction temperature, and time also were optimized systematically, and the optimal reaction conditions for considering peroxidase-like activity and photoluminescence performance meanwhile were 1:1 of mass ratio, 180 ℃ of temperature, and 2 h of reaction time, respectively (Fig. S2).

The morphology, crystalline state, and surface functional groups of Fe,N-CDs were characterized roundly. The TEM image shows that the Fe,N-CDs was spherical particles smaller than 10 nm, and the lattice spacing is about 0.21 nm (Fig. 1 A). From Fig. 1B, we can see that the size of Fe,N-CDs ranges from 3 nm to 10 nm with an average diameter of 5 nm. It can be seen from the XRD pattern (Fig. 1C) that the Fe,N-CDs exhibit a broad peak at 26°, which is allocated to the (002) plane of graphite carbon [27]. The functional groups' information of synthesized Fe,N-CDs was shown in Fig. 1D. The typical absorption at 3450 cm−1, 1645 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 should be ascribed to the stretching vibration of N-H, C = O, and C-N, respectively [28,29]. Besides, we also can find the characteristic peaks of Fe-N and Fe-O at 1320 cm−1 and 575 cm−1, which confirms the presence of Fe in Fe,N-CDs [30,31]. The surface chemical structure of Fe,N-CDs was investigated by XPS, which further proves the existence of the elements of C, N, O and Fe in Fe,N-CDs (Fig. S3). The content of C, N, O and Fe in Fe,N-CDs is about 52.71 %, 28.46 %, 17.19 %, and 1.64 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of Fe,N-CDs (Fig. 1E) shows the presence of C-C (284.8 eV), C-N (286.1 eV), and O = C-O (288.2 eV) [32]. In Fig. 1F, the peaks of Fe-N (398.9 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.9 eV) and graphitic-N (400.8 eV) can be found clearly [33]. The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum indicates the emergence of Fe-O (531.1 eV) and C = O (531.7 eV) (Fig. 1G) [34]. Peaks at 708.3 and 722.4 eV belong to Fe2+ species in Fe,N-CDs while peaks at 710.8 and 725.9 eV belong to Fe3+ species (Fig. 1H) [27]. Therefore, we speculate that the Fe is dispersed on the carbon core and abundant oxygen-/nitrogen-containing functional groups located at the surface of the carbon core (Fig. S4), which generates amounts of Fe-N/O active centers [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like activity and enzyme kinetics of Fe,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of Fe,N-CDs was verified through the typical reaction of TMB-H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the Fe,N-CDs cannot oxidize the TMB to oxTMB directly, while the featuring characteristic peaks at 652 nm of oxTMB can be observed clearly with the simultaneous presence of H2O2 and Fe,N-CDs. Similar to the case of TMB, OPD also can be oxidized to yellow products with absorption at 450 nm, which further confirms the generation of reactive oxygen species in the process of catalyzing H2O2 by Fe,N-CDs (Fig. S5) [36]. The characteristic electron spin-resonance signals of TEMP-1O2, DMPO-⋅OH, and DMPO-O2⋅– in Fig. 2B indicates confirmed the existence of 1O2, ⋅OH, and O2⋅–, respectively [37]. Furthermore, ROS scavenging experiments were performed to roughly analyze the relative amounts of three free radicals. As shown in Fig. S6A, the salicylic acid (SA) can capture ·OH effectively to form the product with absorption at 500 nm increased [38]. In Fig. S6B, the O2⋅- can be recognized easily by p-benzoquinone (PBQ) in the system of Fe,N-CDs-H2O2-TMB, while only a few amounts of 1O2 can be monitored by using sodium azide (NaN3) as scavenger [39,40]. The above results suggest that the main reactive oxygen species in the proposed system are ⋅OH and O2⋅-.

The reaction conditions of Fe,N-CDs-H2O2-TMB system also was optimized as shown in Fig. S7, the optimum reaction concentration of Fe,N-CDs, TMB and H2O2 is 20 μg⋅mL−1, 0.09 mM and 5.0 mM, respectively. Steady-state kinetic measurements were carried out on TMB and H2O2 as substrates using Fe,N-CDs as catalysts. V max and K m values for the Fe,N-CDs-H2O2-TMB system were calculated and plotted by fitting the data to the Michaelis-Menten equation and Lineweaver-Burk double-reciprocal plot [41]. As shown in Fig. 2C and D, using TMB as substrate, the V max and K m of Fe,N-CDs is 2.23 × 10-7 M·s−1 and 0.15 mM, respectively, and the V max and K m of Fe,N-CDs can be calculated as 1.42 × 10-7 M·s−1 and 1.02 mM by using as H2O2 substrate (Fig. 2E,F). The higher V max and lower K m values compared with a series of previously reported results prove the good peroxidase-like activity of Fe,N-CDs (Table S1). The stability of CDs-based nanozyme is very essential for evaluating their applications such as in the biological field. As shown in Fig. S8A, Fe,N-CDs were dissolved in water and tested the enzyme catalytic activity. The results showed the enzyme catalytic activity remained above 85 % after eight days. Secondly, it was found that after storing Fe,N-CDs in air for seven weeks, it exhibited excellent enzyme stability (Fig. S8B). Heat continuously for half an hour at temperatures ranging from 20 to 80 ℃ and return to room temperature, recording enzyme activity currently (Fig. S8C). The corresponding enzyme activity exhibits good enzyme stability after heating.

The optical properties of Fe,N-CDs aqueous solution were characterized firstly. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the Fe,N-CDs have a weak absorption peak at 350 nm, which is associated with n-π* transition [42]. The Fe,N-CDs aqueous solution can emit bright blue fluorescence under UV light excitation with a quantum yield of 10.78 % (quinine sulfate as reference), and the optimal excitation and emission wavelength of that is 392 nm and 456 nm, respectively. The Fe,N-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent characteristics, i.e., the fluorescence emission wavelength of Fe,N-CDs red-shifts gradually with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 3B). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence of Fe,N-CDs exhibits well tolerance to high ion strength environment, light irradiation, and temperature change. These results indicate that the proposed Fe,N-CDs have good optical stability, which lays a solid foundation for further analysis application.

Based on the excellent fluorescence performance and peroxidase-like activity, ratio-absorption and fluorescent dual-mode analysis methods for DA detection were constructed. As shown in Fig. 3C, DA and TMB cannot be oxidized directly by Fe,N-CDs, and the solutions are colorless. TMB can be converted to blue oxTMB with absorption of 652 nm in the presence of Fe,N-CDs and H2O2, but the DA can inhibit this process obviously by competing with TMB for ROS. Besides, the DA also can be oxidized to indole-type polymers with a broad absorption of 350 nm-550 nm by Fe,N-CDs-H2O2 [14,43–45], as shown in Fig. S10. As a result, after the addition of DA, the absorbance at 652 nm decreases and the absorbance at 450 nm increases with the color of the system changes from blue to yellow-green (Fig. 3C), which lays a good foundation for the analysis of DA. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 3D, the addition of H2O2 or DA cannot cause the fluorescence signal change of Fe,N-CDs, while the oxidative products of DA can quench the fluorescence of Fe,N-CDs effectively with the emission intensity at 456 nm decrease. The quenching mechanism also was discussed based on the spectral and fluorescence lifetime characterization results. As given in Fig. S11, the excitation and emission spectra of Fe,N-CDs overlap significantly with the absorption spectrum of the indole-type polymers produced by the DA oxidation, and resulting in an obvious inner filter effect [46,47]. Furthermore, the average fluorescence lifetime of Fe,N-CDs-H2O2 before and after adding DA was 11.51 ns and 6.48 ns, respectively (Fig.S12 and Table S2), suggesting a dynamic quenching process and enabling the effective energy transfer mechanism [48]. Therefore, the synergistic effect of the inner filter effect and the energy transfer caused by dynamic quenching should be considered the main mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of Fe,N-CDs provoked by DA oxidation products.

Based on the competitiveness of DA and TMB to ROS and the quenching effect of oxidized DA to Fe,N-CDs, we constructed a multi-mode analysis system of DA successfully. For UV–Vis ratiometric-absorption analysis, as shown in Fig. 4 A, with increasing the DA concentrations, the absorbance of 652 nm and 450 nm decreased and increased, respectively. The ratio value of A(450nm)/A(652nm) was used to describe the absorption signal change caused by DA. In Fig. 4B, the DA concentrations and A(450nm)/A(652nm) values exhibit a good linear relationship in the range of 0.05–30.0 μM with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9989. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be about 15 nM. In fluorescence analysis, under 370 nm excitation, the fluorescence intensities of Fe,N-CDs-H2O2 decreased gradually with the increase of DA concentrations (Fig. 4D). As shown in Fig. 4E, in the range of 0.04–30.0 μM, the fluorescence response was linearly correlated with DA concentrations (r = 0.9961), and the LOD was can reach 10 nM based on the triple signal-to-noise ratio.

Moreover, the excellent color change of the proposed system sparked us to develop scanometric analysis method for realizing simple and rapid detection. High-resolution images of Fe,N-CDs-H2O2-TMB solutions with different concentrations of DA presence are captured by Smartphone and the RGB values were converted using the “Image J.” program (Fig. 4G). From Fig. 4H we can see that the solutions' color change from blue to green after adding the DA, in the concentration range of 0.5–30.0 μM, the G/B values has a good linear relationship with DA concentrations. The LOD is about 0.1 μM with r = 0.9926 (Fig. 4H). As shown in Table S3, the linear ranges and LODs of the developed multi-mode detection method of DA are comparable or even superior to previous reports. All the above results indicate that the proposed UV–Vis ratiometric absorption, fluorescence quenching and scanometric analysis system can be applied for the sensitive determination of DA.

Selectivity is an important indicator to evaluate the reliability of the established multi-mode detection system. As shown in Fig. 4C, only DA can cause a clear change of A(450nm)/A(652nm) values, which should be ascribed to the unique optical characteristic of DA oxidative product. Other common organic compounds or inorganic ions cannot lead to a distinct interference with DA detection. Furthermore, in Fig. 4F, DA shows a superior quenching effect to Fe,N-CDs compared with other potential interfering substances. For scanometric analysis, we can see from the inset in Fig. 4I that only DA can lead to an intense color transform with the greatest change of G/B values, which proves its feasibility in simple field detection. The above results reveal that the constructed multi-mode detection methods of DA possess excellent specificity and are suitable for practical detection.

Medical dopamine hydrochloride injection with certified concentration was selected as standard samples to verify the accuracy of developed methods. From Table S4 we can see that the detected concentrations are all close to the standard certified concentrations, and the recoveries range of 96.8 %-106.0 %, indicating the well practicability of the developed methods. Real sample detection was carried out to investigate the availability of the proposed multi-mode analysis, including fetal bovine serum, calf serum, eggs and quail eggs. Recovery experiments were performed at the DA concentrations of 1.0, 10, and 20 μM. As listed in Table 1 , scanometric analysis mode was used for the simple and rapid detection of DA in real samples. The recoveries of the proposed method are in the range of 89.0–112.9 % with RSDs less than 7.0 %. Furthermore, the recoveries of UV–Vis ratiometric-absorption mode and fluorescent mode are 95.3 %-113.0 % and 94.1 %-118.0 %, respectively, and relative standard deviations (RSDs) are all lower than 5 % (Table S5). These results suggest that the developed multi-mode analysis system is useful and reliable for DA detection.

Thanks to the excellent fluorescence properties of Fe,N-CDs and their special response to DA, Fe,N-CDs are further used for intracellular imaging of DA. Firstly, in Fig. S13, cells can still maintain activity of over 80 % even when the Fe,N-CDs concentration reaches 500 μg·mL−1, indicating that the designed CDs-based nanozyme has good biocompatibility. As shown in Fig. 5 , Fe,N-CDs can be uniformly dispersed into cells and exhibit bright blue fluorescence under UV light excitation. In the presence of H2O2, the blue fluorescence in the intracellular region gradually decreases with increasing DA concentration. It is proved that the synthesized CDs-based nanozyme can be used as an effective sensor for the intracellular imaging analysis of DA.

In summary, we developed a novel Fe,N-CDs nanozyme with excellent peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence performance through an effective solid-phase synthesis strategy, which provides an efficient route for the synthesis of bifunctional nanozymes. The proposed Fe,N-CDs can emit bright blue fluorescence and catalytic H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species. Based on the unique oxidation effect of reactive oxygen species to DA, a multi-mode DA detection platform of naked eye colorimetric, UV–Vis ratiometric-absorption, fluorescence quenching, and scanometric method, was fabricated successfully, which effectively overcomes the disadvantages of poor selectivity of traditional methods. The constructed methods were successfully applied for the DA analysis in food and biological samples with a reliable recovery. We believe that this study may provide a valuable reference for the preparation of nanozymes and the construction of a multi-mode analysis system, which can promote the process of replacing natural enzymes with artificial enzymes.

Hua Wei: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Hongli Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Yutong Zhang: Methodology. Xin Wang: Visualization, Conceptualization. Zihan Li: Software, Resources. Mingyang Zhou: Supervision, Software. Yongqian Lei: Visualization. Pengran Guo: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jian Liu: Project administration, Conceptualization. Shenghong Yang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.22466021, 22106054), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (Nos.ZR2024QB057, ZR2022MC153), the Lanzhou Science and Technology Planning Project (No.2023-2-77), the Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. 20224BAB203029). Research Fund Program of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Measurement and Emergency Test Technology (No.KF2024004), the GDAS’ Project of Science and Technology Development (No.2023GDASZH-2023010103).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112597.

ce:section.ce:para:
